## PubMed Articles

### The role of resistance training and creatine supplementation on oxidative stress, antioxidant defense, muscle strength, and quality of life in older adults.
**Authors:** Amiri E, Sheikholeslami-Vatani D
**Journal:** Front Public Health, 2023, **11**, 1062832
**Type:** Randomized Controlled Trial, Journal Article
**PMID:** 37206869
**DOI:** 10.3389/fpubh.2023.1062832
**PubMed:** https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37206869/
**PMC:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10189876/

#### Abstract
BACKGROUND: The aim of this study was to evaluate the effect of resistance training (RT) with creatine monohydrate supplementation (CS) on serum levels of(8-OHdG), malondialdehyde (MDA), glutathione peroxidase (GPX), and total antioxidant capacity (TAC) in older adults.

OBJECTIVES: This study evaluated the effect of resistance training with creatine monohydrate supplementation on oxidative stress and antioxidant defense, muscle strength and quality of life in older adults.

METHODS: We examined 45 non-athlete volunteer older men and women (mean, 68.1 ± 7.2  years old), were randomly selected and divided into three groups of 15: RT with creatine supplementation (RT + CS), RT with placebo (RT + P) and control group. RT protocol was performed for 10  weeks, three sessions per week. Creatine supplement was taken daily at a dose of 0.1 g/kg of body weight, while the placebo group consumed the same amount of starch. Fasting blood samples were taken before the start of program and at the end of the RT period.

RESULTS: In the training groups, after 10  weeks of RT, a significant decrease in MDA and 8 - OHDG as well as a significant increase in serum levels of GPX and TAC were observed (in all cases,=  0.001). In addition, creatinine levels were enhanced in the RT + CS (=  0.014). Training intervention also improved quality of life and muscle strength in the experimental groups (=  0.001), although muscle strength changes were more visible in the RT + CS group than in the RT + P group (<  0/05).

CONCLUSION: Regular resistance training can be recommended as a very suitable non-pharmacological approach to strengthen the body's antioxidant system, muscle strength and quality of life in older adults. There are no definite findings on the role of creatine on the antioxidant system and quality of life in older adults, but the use of this supplement in addition to RT can double the amount of strength gained from resistance training.

Conclusion: Regular resistance training can be recommended as a very suitable non-pharmacological approach to strengthen the body’s antioxidant system, muscle strength and quality of life in older adults. #### Introduction
The production of free radicals increases from the fourth decade of life onwards, and the amount of antioxidant enzymes such as superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione peroxidase (GPX) decreases (). Reactive oxygen species (ROS) lead to oxidative damage in lipid membranes and DNA through programmed cell death (). Lipid peroxidation produces substances such as malondialdehyde (MDA) (), and DNA damage is measured through(8-OHdG) (). One of the factors that plays an important role in the loss of an active lifestyle is the progressive reduction of muscle mass or sarcopenia (). Muscle mass forms more than 50% of the body weight of young people, while this amount reaches approximately 25% in older adults (). 1 2 3 4 5 6 8-hydroxydeoxyguanosine

Cumulative damage to skeletal muscle and nerve cells in sarcopenia may result from oxidative stress. Sarcopenia could be caused by an increase of endogenous ROS formation in skeletal muscle but the source of ROS in sarcopenic muscle is still relatively unknown. However, an age-associated increase of ROS levels in muscle mass, as a consequence of an upregulation of NADPH oxidase 2 enzyme (NOX), has been reported (). Moreover, a study by Sullivan-Gunn and Lewandowski () has highlighted the role of NOXenzyme in a healthy mouse model of aging, suggesting that elevated levels of HOfrom NOX, as well as the lack of antioxidant protection from catalase and glutathione peroxidase (GPX), carry out a key role in the onset of sarcopenia. The lack of SODalso causes a reduction of skeletal muscle mass, impairment of neurotransmitter release, and neuronal degeneration in mice (). 2 2 2 2 2 1 7 8 9

Moreover, irrespective of the mechanism, oxidative stress causes the onset of many types of disease such as cardiovascular disease (CVD) and cancer (,) as well as modulation of cancer treatment-related outcomes (,). Excessive ROS levels have been linked to tumor initiation, growth and progression (). As mentioned before, the production of ROS increases with age. Therefore, the possibility of getting diseases related to ROS increases in older adults. Therapeutic benefits of creatine supplementation (CS) in some diseases associated with oxidative stress have been confirmed (,). 10 11 12 13 14 15 16

In the body as a whole, creatine is synthesized in the kidney and in the liver (). Specifically, the kidney accomplishes the first step of the synthesis, forming guanidinoacetic acid from arginine and glycine. Guanidinoacetic acid is then transported to the liver, where it is converted into creatine with the intervention of the methyl donor S-adenosyl-methionine (). Creatine monohydrate is a common and popular supplement used by many athletes to improve strength, endurance and athletic performance (). The antioxidant properties of creatine might be due to the presence of arginine, which disintegrates nitric oxide (). The NO formed undergoes oxidative degradation to the stable inorganic nitrogen oxides, nitrite (NO), and nitrate (NO), which are detectable in plasma and urine (). 17 17 18 19 20 2 3

Resistance training (RT) has the beneficial effects of preventing the complications of aging and leads to improved health through a variety of mechanisms (). RT has been shown to reduce MDA and hydrogen peroxidase, while boosting GPX and SOD activity (). On the other hand, in aging, we face a phenomenon called anabolic resistance which reduces the response of muscles to RT (). 21 22 23

Long-term creatine supplementation along with moderate-intensity resistance and endurance training can probably reduce oxidative stress and increase the antioxidant defense syste

fulltext_summary.title: The role of resistance training and creatine supplementation on oxidative stress, antioxidant defense, muscle strength, and quality of life in older adults
fulltext_summary.study_type: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group trial (3 arms), 10-week intervention
fulltext_summary.population: 45 community-dwelling older adults (men and women), age range 60–70 years (N=45; 15 per group), non-athletes, recruited in Kermanshah, Iran
fulltext_summary.intervention: Resistance training (RT) 3×/week for 10 weeks (70-min sessions; 3 sets × 10 reps at ~75% 1‑RM across 7 exercises) plus creatine monohydrate 0.1 g/kg/day taken daily (single dose post-exercise or at 6 pm on non-training days)
fulltext_summary.comparator: (1) Resistance training + placebo (maltodextrin, identical dosing/administration) and (2) Control group (no intervention)
key_findings: Resistance training (both RT+creatine and RT+placebo) for 10 weeks significantly reduced oxidative damage markers (malondialdehyde [MDA] and 8‑OHdG) and increased antioxidant defenses (glutathione peroxidase [GPX] and total antioxidant capacity [TAC]).
No additional reduction in MDA or 8‑OHdG was observed with creatine versus placebo; TAC increases were similar between RT groups in post-test comparisons.
GPX increased in both RT groups, but the increase was greater in the RT+creatine group than RT+placebo (post-test difference, p≈0.004).

Muscle strength improved significantly in both RT groups across seven movements; mean strength gain averaged ~36% in RT+placebo and ~57% in RT+creatine, with RT+creatine showing significantly larger gains than RT+placebo.
Quality of life (SF‑36) improved in both RT groups (reported ~80% improvement RT+placebo and ~89% RT+creatine) compared with control.
Body mass index decreased significantly only in the RT+placebo group.
Serum creatinine increased significantly in the RT+creatine group after 10 weeks (post‑intervention rise, p≈0.014); no between‑group difference in creatinine at post-test was reported.
limitations: Small sample size (N=15 per arm), single‑center study limiting statistical power and generalizability.
Relatively short intervention (10 weeks) — long‑term effects and durability of gains not assessed.
No direct muscle tissue measurements of oxidative markers (only serum measures); nitric oxide metabolites were not measured.
Renal safety assessment limited to serum creatinine; no reported eGFR or other kidney function measures and clinical significance of creatinine rise unclear.
Participants were healthy, motivated, community‑dwelling older adults (exclusion of comorbid/medicated individuals) which limits applicability to frailer or multimorbid older populations.
Some reporting inconsistencies in descriptive statistics (age reported differently in sections) and limited detail on adherence/compliance beyond attendance criteria.
fulltext_summary.practical_takeaway: Supervised resistance training (3×/week at ~75% 1‑RM) for 10 weeks improves antioxidant capacity, reduces oxidative damage, increases muscle strength and quality of life in older adults. Adding daily creatine monohydrate at 0.1 g/kg/day amplified strength gains (substantially larger increases versus RT+placebo) and produced a greater rise in GPX but did not further lower serum oxidative damage markers or TAC beyond RT alone. Creatinine rose with creatine supplementation, so clinicians should monitor renal function if prescribing creatine to older adults; larger, longer trials with fuller safety assessments are needed before broad clinical adoption.
